title: Sudden tearing belly and back pain with near-collapse

chart.cc: Sudden severe abdominal and back pain
chart.oneLiner: 74M smoker with hypertension presents with sudden, severe, tearing abdominal and flank/back pain and a near-syncopal episode.

opening: It hit me all of a sudden — a tearing pain in my belly that goes straight through to my back, and I nearly passed out.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 74-year-old man, pale, clammy, and clearly unwell. Anxious but cooperative.

HPI: SUDDEN onset ~1h ago of severe, constant, tearing/ripping abdominal pain radiating to the flank and back. Felt lightheaded and nearly fainted when standing. NO trauma. NO vomiting blood or black stools. NO fever or urinary symptoms. He thinks he may have felt a 'pulsing' lump in his belly over recent months but never had it checked (only if asked about abdominal swelling/lumps or prior screening).
PMH: long-standing hypertension (poorly controlled), known COPD, hyperlipidemia, peripheral arterial disease. He recalls being told years ago of a 'bulge on the main blood vessel' that needed watching but missed follow-up (only if asked about aneurysm/prior scans/screening). MEDS: amlodipine (admits poor adherence), atorvastatin, albuterol inhaler. SOCIAL: 50 pack-year smoker, still smoking; occasional alcohol. FAMILY: father died of a 'burst blood vessel in the belly' (only if asked about family history).

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: pale, diaphoretic, in distress, restless.
- Vitals: BP 88/54, HR 122, RR 24, 36.7°C, SpO2 95% RA.
- Abdomen: a pulsatile, expansile midline/periumbilical mass with tenderness; mild distension; bowel sounds present but reduced. No rebound or rigidity early.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular, no murmur.
- Extremities: cool with weak/diminished and asymmetric femoral and distal pulses; prolonged capillary refill; possible flank/periumbilical ecchymosis.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis. Answer only what is asked.
- Do NOT volunteer the prior aneurysm/'bulge' history or the pulsing lump unless asked about aneurysms, prior scans/screening, or abdominal swelling.
- Do NOT volunteer the family history of a 'burst blood vessel' unless asked about family history.
- The pulsatile expansile mass, hypotension/tachycardia, and asymmetric pulses are key — reveal the mass only if the abdomen is palpated and the pulses only if examined.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.POCUS_aorta: Bedside ultrasound (FAST/aorta): infrarenal abdominal aorta dilated to ~7 cm with surrounding/retroperitoneal free fluid — diagnostic of a leaking/ruptured AAA.
labs.CBC: Hgb 9.8 (dropping from baseline), WBC 14.0, Plt 240 — consistent with acute blood loss.
labs.BMP: Cr 1.6 (acute kidney injury from hypoperfusion); electrolytes otherwise notable for evolving lactic acidosis.
labs.Coags_Type: Type & crossmatch sent; coagulation panel drawn to guide massive transfusion. INR mildly elevated.
labs.CTA_abdomen: CT angiography (only if hemodynamically stabilizable): infrarenal AAA ~7 cm with active contrast extravasation and retroperitoneal hematoma — confirms rupture and maps anatomy for repair.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: Sinus tachycardia at ~120 bpm; no acute ST-elevation or ischemic changes — a compensatory response to hypovolemia, not a primary cardiac event.
ekg.verify: From the LITFL 'Sinus Tachycardia' library page — download an example tracing that matches: Sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemic changes (compensatory to hypovolemia) and self-host with credit to litfl.com.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.
ekg.imageDescription: 12-lead showing sinus tachycardia without acute ischemic changes

cxr.label: Chest X-ray (upright)
cxr.findings: No acute cardiopulmonary process and NO free air under the diaphragm; mediastinum not widened — helps exclude perforated viscus and thoracic aortic catastrophe rather than diagnose the AAA.
cxr.verify: Pick a CC normal upright chest film (no subdiaphragmatic free air, no mediastinal widening).
cxr.imageDescription: upright chest radiograph, no free air, normal mediastinum
searchTerms: chest radiograph no free air no mediastinal widening

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your history and physical, list your differential diagnosis (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm (leading & CANT-MISS,9; accepts ruptured/leaking AAA). Aortic dissection (CANT-MISS,4). Perforated viscus (CANT-MISS,3). Renal colic / nephrolithiasis (core,2). Acute mesenteric ischemia (core,2). Names ruptured AAA with the key clue — sudden tearing pain + pulsatile mass + hypotension in an elderly smoker (core,5).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Aortic dissection; Perforated viscus. Also reasonable: Renal colic / nephrolithiasis; Acute mesenteric ischemia.

coreActions.item: Names ruptured AAA with the key clue — sudden tearing pain + pulsatile mass + hypotension in an elderly smoker

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What initial diagnostic workup do you want to order?
steps.rubric: Bedside aortic ultrasound / POCUS (CANT-MISS,5). Type & crossmatch + activate massive transfusion (CANT-MISS,4). CT angiography of the abdomen if hemodynamically stabilizable (3). CBC (2). BMP / lactate for perfusion (1). PENALTY: sending an unstable patient to CT or delaying repair for non-essential imaging (-3); shotgun panel w/ no reasoning (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Order: EKG; CXR; Pocus aorta; CBC; BMP; Lactate; Coags type; CTA abdomen.

penalties.item: sending an unstable patient to CT or delaying definitive repair for non-essential imaging

contraindicatedTests: sending an unstable patient to CT or delaying definitive repair for non-essential imaging
orderSensitiveActions: sending an unstable patient to CT or delaying definitive repair for non-essential imaging

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: identifies sinus tachycardia (7); recognizes it as compensatory to hypovolemia/hemorrhage rather than a primary cardiac event (4); no acute STEMI (2); systematic read (2). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia / treating as ACS (-3).
keyFindings: recognizes it as compensatory to hypovolemia/hemorrhage
criticalFindings: Sinus tachycardia at ~120 bpm; no acute ST-elevation or ischemic changes — a compensatory response to hypovolemia, not a primary cardiac event.
commonMistakes: over-calling ischemia and treating this as ACS
steps.idealAnswer: Sinus tachycardia at ~120 bpm; no acute ST-elevation or ischemic changes — a compensatory response to hypovolemia, not a primary cardiac event.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: Sinus tachycardia at ~120 bpm; no acute ST-elevation or ischemic changes — a compensatory response to hypovolemia, not a primary cardiac event.

coreActions.item: recognizes it as compensatory to hypovolemia/hemorrhage

penalties.item: over-calling ischemia and treating this as ACS

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process; no free air; mediastinum not widened. CREDIT: identifies a normal/nonspecific film (5); explicitly looks for and excludes free air (perforation) and a widened mediastinum (thoracic aortic catastrophe) (5). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: excludes free air and a widened mediastinum
criticalFindings: No acute cardiopulmonary process and NO free air under the diaphragm; mediastinum not widened — helps exclude perforated viscus and thoracic aortic catastrophe rather than diagnose the AAA.
commonMistakes: inventing pathology or expecting the CXR to show the AAA
steps.idealAnswer: No acute cardiopulmonary process and NO free air under the diaphragm; mediastinum not widened — helps exclude perforated viscus and thoracic aortic catastrophe rather than diagnose the AAA.

criticalActions.item: Recognizes: No acute cardiopulmonary process and NO free air under the diaphragm; mediastinum not widened — helps exclude perforated viscus and thoracic aortic catastrophe rather than diagnose the AAA.

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: Given the hypotension, dropping hemoglobin, and a bedside ultrasound showing a 7 cm aorta with free fluid, what is your diagnosis and reasoning?
steps.rubric: Ruptured (leaking) abdominal aortic aneurysm (6). Links sudden tearing pain + pulsatile mass + hypotension + POCUS findings (dilated aorta with free/retroperitoneal fluid); recognizes this as a surgical emergency and excludes dissection/perforation (4).
steps.idealAnswer: Ruptured (leaking) abdominal aortic aneurysm — the sudden tearing pain with a pulsatile abdominal mass and hypotension in an elderly smoker, plus a bedside POCUS showing a 7 cm aorta with free fluid and a dropping hemoglobin, is diagnostic. This is a surgical emergency; dissection and perforated viscus are excluded.

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH abdominal/vascular emergency ('can't-miss' ruptured AAA): immediate STAT vascular surgery consult for emergent open or endovascular (EVAR) repair (CANT-MISS,7); activate massive transfusion / balanced blood products with permissive hypotension (CANT-MISS,6); large-bore IV access and type-specific/uncrossmatched blood (4); avoid over-resuscitation — target permissive hypotension (SBP ~70-90 / mentating) until repair (4); NPO, continuous monitoring, two large-bore IVs, OR activation (2); analgesia and reverse anticoagulation/coagulopathy (2). PENALTY: aggressive crystalloid/large-volume fluid bolus driving up BP and worsening hemorrhage (-4); discharging or delaying surgery for additional imaging in an unstable patient (-3).
steps.idealAnswer: Surgical emergency. Immediate STAT vascular surgery consult for emergent open or endovascular (EVAR) repair. Two large-bore IVs, type & crossmatch, activate massive transfusion protocol with balanced blood products. Practice PERMISSIVE HYPOTENSION (target SBP ~70-90 or mentating) and avoid aggressive crystalloid that raises BP and worsens bleeding. Reverse any coagulopathy/anticoagulation, provide analgesia, keep NPO, continuous monitoring, and move to the OR. Do NOT delay definitive repair for non-essential imaging in an unstable patient.

criticalActions.item: STAT vascular surgery consult for emergent open / EVAR repair
Activate massive transfusion / balanced blood products

coreActions.item: Large-bore IV access and type-specific/uncrossmatched blood
Permissive hypotension (target SBP ~70-90 / mentating) until repair

penalties.item: Aggressive crystalloid bolus raising BP and worsening hemorrhage

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 74M smoker with hypertension presents with sudden, severe, tearing abdominal and flank/back pain and a near-syncopal episode. The diagnosis is Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Abdomen: a pulsatile, expansile midline/periumbilical mass with tenderness; mild distension; bowel sounds present but reduced. No rebound or rigidity early.
Abdomen: a pulsatile, expansile midline/periumbilical mass with tenderness; mild distension; bowel sounds present but reduced. No rebound or rigidity early.
Extremities: cool with weak/diminished and asymmetric femoral and distal pulses; prolonged capillary refill; possible flank/periumbilical ecchymosis.
General: pale, diaphoretic, in distress, restless.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
commonPitfalls: Aggressive crystalloid bolus raising BP and worsening hemorrhage
Delaying definitive repair to obtain non-essential imaging in an unstable patient
managementPearls: STAT vascular surgery consult for emergent open / EVAR repair
Activate massive transfusion / balanced blood products
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia at ~120 bpm; no acute ST-elevation or ischemic changes — a compensatory response to hypovolemia, not a primary cardiac event.
No acute cardiopulmonary process and NO free air under the diaphragm; mediastinum not widened — helps exclude perforated viscus and thoracic aortic catastrophe rather than diagnose the AAA.

Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.
